turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Para, this for, para, can also mean in order to, in order to. So in a sentence, for example, We have spoken in order to resolve it. We have spoken in order to resolve it. Now in English we could just, no? We don’t need to say say We have spoken to resolve it in order to. But whenever we have this to meaning in order to, we want to translate this as para in Spanish. So let’s make this sentence. First bit is We have spoken. We have spoken:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Hemos hablado. To resolve it – in order to resolve it. Now, to resolve is resolver. Ah? The same word, resolver. So We have spoken in order to resolve it:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Hemos hablado para resolverlo. Good. So whilst the to of to resolve, resolver, would fit nicely here, I mean if we were to translate directly from English, We have spoken to resolve it, it doesn’t work in Spanish. Whenever this to means in order to, we must put that para. Hemos hablado para resolverlo. You could say We have spoken in order to try to resolve it. What was to try? Related to intention:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. We have spoken in order to try to resolve it:

turns.speaker: student
expected_answers: Hemos hablado para intentar resolvar– resolverlo.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Hemos hablado para intentar resolverlo. So only when the to implies in order to we want. The “to” to put para form of the verb in Spanish is not enough to include that meaning. We must I’m here to see you I’m here in order put para. So for example, if you want to say, does it mean to see you?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. So I am here to see you (informally):

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Estoy aquí para verte. This is also something we could learn or interiorize through the negative language transfer that some Spanish speakers make when they speak English. For example, you might hear a Spanish speaker say I am here for see you or for seeing you. You might hear a Spanish speaker, especially with basic English, making this mistake. Now once you’ve heard that a few times, you would say, “Oh, maybe this is how you say it in Spanish.” So we don’t want to forget this opportunity, the opportunity to learn from the negative language transfer, the mistakes that Spanish speakers make in English, which originate from Spanish. Once you hear the same mistake a few times, you can assume it probably comes from Spanish. I have called you in order to speak about it: so the first bit is I have called you, I have phoned, I have called (informally):

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: In order to speak about it:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: No. Good. Te he llamado para hablarlo. So if we take the sentence in English, I have called you to speak about it, and we translate it directly, then we will say Te he llamado hablarlo. But when our to means in order to, we must put this para. Te he llamado para hablarlo. I have called you in order to speak about it. Good. What was to be, for states?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Estar. And we said that we can use this to express when we are doing something right now. We can say, instead of I eat, como, I am eating, using this structure with estar and then the - ing form of the verb. Do you remember how you would get the - ing form of comer, for example? Of – er or – ir verbs?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Comiendo.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Comiendo. So we add on - iendo for verbs and for – ar verbs we add on?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. So how would you say I am eating?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Estoy comiendo. And this means right now, no? I am speaking:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Estoy hablando. I am explaining it: to explain – we would go from explanation but explanation is an exception. It’s not “explanación” in Spanish as we might hope, it’s explicación. And we see we even have that / k / sound in explain, explicación, we have it in English as well in inexplicable, for example we say in English. Inexplicable in Spanish. So we can see where that sound’s coming from. So how is explanation or explication in Spanish?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. And to explain:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Explicar. Good. So and you mean right now?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Estoy explicándolo. Lo estoy explicando. The lo can go on the end of a “to” form, but it can also go on the end of one of these - ando or - iendo forms as well. So you have the option. Lo estoy explicando. Estoy explicándolo. And you mean right now. Actually in explicar, we get this - plicar from “plicare“ that we mentioned before, meaning to fold in Latin. So explicar, to I’m taking a Spanish course. Even if you’re not in the explain, to unfold. You might want to say course right now in this moment, when we are taking a course, in our minds there’s a period of I’m time in which we are taking a course and when we say it we’re in that period of time. If I say, maybe I mean for three months and I’m in that period. Do you remember taking a Spanish course what to take was? We used it with to take a photo. Tomar.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Tomar. So tomar is to take. So I am taking:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Estoy tomando. A course

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Close. It’s not with, it’s with a o.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Un curso, with a u. Un curso. Good. Of Spanish, this is how you would say a Spanish course, a course of Spanish.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Very good. Estoy tomando un curso de español. Good. Now, what if we put this estoy in the past tense that we learnt? The past tense that is - aba or - ía. I was taking a Spanish course:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good.

turns.speaker: student
expected_answers: Estaba tomando un curso de español.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Estaba tomando un curso de español. Which means we actually have two ways of saying I was taking. We can say estaba tomando or we can just use tomar and use that line in the past. How would that sound?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Yes. So both tomaba and estaba tomando mean I was taking. So you have that option. Estaba tomando un curso de español or tomaba un curso de español. What was to eat?

turns.text: Comer. How would you say I was eating?

turns.speaker: tutor

turns.speaker: tutor

So if in one situation people tend to use estaba comiendo rather than comía, you will pick it up. So we have - ando and - iendo to form this - ing. And we saw in Spanish that we don’t use it after prepositions. You don’t use this - ing either if you say something. You don’t use the - like swimming is healthy or running is healthy ing there either. You will just say to run is healthy, to swim is healthy. If you hear people toast in Spanish you might hear them say salud, which means health. Healthy is saludable. To dance is healthy:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Bailar es saludable. Good. To swim is nadar. Nadar. To swim is healthy:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Nadar es saludable. So we’re seeing that we don’t use the - ing in Spanish as much as we do in English. Sometimes in English we make an adjective with - ing. We go from to interest to interesting. When we do this in English, this - ing that we use in English to form an adjective, in Spanish it’s - –er/-ir ante for – ar verbs and - iente for verbs. So for example, we have to interest, what was to interest?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Interesar. So if you get rid of - ar and you put - ante, you get that - ing adjective, interesting. So how would you say interesting?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Interesante. Good. So when you make an adjective with - ing in English from the verb, you can just replace it with - ante or - iente. How would you say to irritate in Spanish? And you can go from irritation:

turns.text: Irritar. So if you want to say irritating:

turns.speaker: tutor

turns.speaker: tutor

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Yes, because you have the two r's, no? Frustrate. Frustrate, Frustrar. Frustrating:

turns.speaker: tutor

turns.speaker: tutor

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. So there we’re making waning like an adjective, not like the verb, not menguando. Ah? Menguante, la luna menguante. Luna is an interesting word. I don’t know if you ever noticed, like, in English Monday is “moon day”. It’s not very obvious in English that the days of the week relate back to the planets. But in Spanish it’s very obvious. You have la luna, the moon, and then you have lunes, Monday. Tuesday is martes. And Mars is marte, planeta marte. Wednesday in Spanish is miércoles. What planet does that sound like?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Yes. Jueves in Spanish is spelt with a j:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Jupiter. Viernes is Friday:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Venus. So you can see much clearer there how our days of the week are named after planets. To live was vivir. V-i-v-i-r. So vivir is to live.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. We have two “to be”s, we have estar for states, and for characteristics we have?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: How do you get this –

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: You could say un ser vivo. Vivo is also like an adjective, which means alive. But living is adjective –er/-ir that we make with - ing. We can build it from vivir. For – ar verbs we have - ante and for verbs we have - iente.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Un ser viviente. A living being. What was to go out or to leave?
